planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring before new growth emerges. Plant divisions in consistently moist or wet soil in a shaded or partly shaded location. Be aware that this plant spreads aggressively by rhizomes and should be sited with containment in mind.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in boggy or reliably moist ground, spacing them generously to accommodate their eventual spread. Choose a site in partial shade near a pond edge, stream bank, or rain garden where the soil rarely dries out. The dramatic pink flower spikes emerge before the leaves in early spring; the enormous leaves that follow provide bold textural interest through summer. Install a buried root barrier if planting near manicured areas, as rhizomes spread vigorously.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in early spring and replant immediately in wet, cold-climate shade. Well-suited to northern and high-elevation gardens where other groundcovers struggle.

directions: Establish divisions in spring in deep shade or heavy shade with consistently wet or waterlogged soil. This native species thrives at the margins of cold streams, bogs, and seeps in northern gardens. It spreads at a moderate pace by rhizomes, forming dense weed-suppressing colonies useful for stabilizing moist, shaded banks. Far less aggressive than its European relative, it makes a manageable groundcover for cold-climate woodland gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow winter cold stratification to break dormancy naturally. Seeds will germinate the following spring. Moist, rich soil in partial shade gives best results.

directions: Sow seeds directly in fall into a prepared bed of moist, humus-rich soil in a partially shaded location. Cold winter temperatures naturally stratify the seeds so they sprout reliably in spring without pre-treatment. Water avens thrives at the edges of woodland ponds, along shaded stream banks, or in rain gardens where soil moisture stays consistently high. The nodding, dusky rose flowers appear in late spring and the decorative feathery seed heads extend the ornamental interest well into summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into gritty, well-drained soil; seeds require cold stratification over winter to germinate in spring. Thin seedlings to final spacing once established.

directions: Direct sow seed in fall into sharply drained, gritty or rocky soil that mimics its alpine home. Cold stratification through winter breaks dormancy naturally, and seedlings emerge in spring once temperatures moderate. This compact perennial prefers cool summers and excellent drainage; it performs best in rock gardens, troughs, or the front of a cool-climate border. Allow the attractive feathery seed heads to develop after the golden flowers fade for continued ornamental interest.

planting.note: Sow seeds directly in fall; cold stratification over winter is essential for reliable germination the following spring. Fresh seed sown immediately after harvest also germinates well. Germination can be slow and uneven.

directions: Direct sow seed in fall into moist, fertile, humus-rich soil in a partially shaded woodland garden or border. The cold of winter naturally stratifies the seed, and germination occurs the following spring; patience is needed as emergence can be slow. Plants reach blooming size in the second or third year, producing their distinctive deep rose-pink pincushion flowers surrounded by decorative pointed bracts from late spring through midsummer. Deadheading prolongs the blooming season, and the plants will self-sow gently once established.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as new growth begins. Replant divisions immediately at the same depth in consistently moist, humus-rich soil. Shelter from afternoon sun to prevent leaf wilting.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in moist to wet, organically rich soil with dappled or partial shade, particularly protection from harsh afternoon sun. Ligularia veitchiana is one of the most dramatically scaled perennials for the moist shade garden, with leaves that can exceed 18 inches across and flower spikes rising 4 to 6 feet in midsummer. Keep soil consistently moist throughout the growing season, as plants will wilt dramatically during dry spells even in shade. This bold specimen works well planted in large groups near water features or as a backdrop plant in sheltered woodland borders.
